result.diagram_code:
sequenceDiagram
    actor I as Attacker
    participant K as KeyReg
    participant A as Initiator A
    participant B as Responder B
    participant S as Server

    I->>K: (B, pk(y))
    Note right of K: insert keys(B, pk(y))

    I->>A: start (A, B)
    A->>S: (A, B)
    S-->>A: sign((pk(y), B), skS)
    A->>I: encrypt((Na, A), pk(y))
    Note over I: decrypt with y and learn Na

    I->>B: start B
    I->>B: encrypt((Na, A), pkB)
    B->>S: (B, A)
    S-->>B: sign((pkA, A), skS)
    B-->>I: encrypt((Na, Nb, B), pkA)

    I->>A: encrypt((Na, Nb, B), pkA)
    A->>I: encrypt(Nb, pk(y))
    Note over I: decrypt with y and learn Nb

    A-->>I: sencrypt(secretANa, Na)
    A-->>I: sencrypt(secretANb, Nb)
    Note over I: use Na and Nb to recover both secrets
result.explanation: The main break is key-table poisoning via !processK. The attacker can register keys(A, pk(y)) or keys(B, pk(y)) for honest names, and processS will then sign these attacker-chosen bindings. In the trace, A accepts a signed certificate binding B to an attacker key, so message 3 is encrypted to the attacker; later A also returns Nb under that same attacker key. This lets the attacker learn Na and Nb and decrypt secretANa/secretANb. The same certificate-substitution pattern also lets responder sessions complete without a matching honest peer, so all authentication queries fail.
result.notes: The file also contains hard-coded uses of skA/skB where skxA/skxB would normally be expected, but the supplied counterexamples already succeed because the attacker can poison the key table for A/B.
